summary: Gruber examines what he calls 'Untitled Document Syndrome' — the tendency to work for hours in a new document without saving it — as a symptom of a deeper problem: friction in software design. He draws an analogy to programming languages, where syntactically lightweight languages like Perl and Python encourage writing small programs that would never get written in verbose languages like C. He argues that the mental overhead of the Save dialog, while minor, is enough to discourage people from creating and storing small bits of information. Using his own experience switching from a folder of text files to Yojimbo, he demonstrates that removing file-management friction dramatically increased his note-taking from once a month to once a day. The core argument is that more software should adopt the library paradigm — abstracting away the file system — because when you reduce friction, people do surprisingly more.
key_points: Friction between having an idea and acting on it — even something as small as a Save dialog — causes people to skip important steps or avoid creating data altogether
Programming languages like Perl and Python succeed partly because they minimize boilerplate, letting you say what you want without saying much before it — and this principle applies to all software
Apps like iTunes and iPhoto succeeded by abstracting file system management into a library paradigm, freeing users from naming and organizing files manually
Gruber's switch from a text-file folder to Yojimbo increased his note-taking from ~1/month to ~1/day, proving that reduced friction leads to dramatically more usage
Arguing that users should embrace manual file system management for all data is arguing against human nature
money_quotes: One thing that's easy about Perl is that you don't have to say much before you say what you want to say.
There's the stuff you want to do, and there's the stuff you have to do before you can do what you want to do.
People have a natural tendency to skip the have to do stuff to get right to the want to do stuff if they can get away with it.
To argue that users should embrace manual file system management for every bit of data they wish to store is to argue against human nature.
When you don't have to do much before (or after) doing what you want to do, you do surprisingly more.
It's like having a mind with an unfailing memory (which I most decidedly do not possess).
Saving a document for the first time is a minor chore, but it's a chore nonetheless. The avoidance of such a minor chore is not rational; but we humans are not perfectly rational.
key_insight: Even trivial friction in software — like a Save dialog — silently suppresses how much people create and store, which is why the best-designed apps abstract away file management entirely.
